intentional-reading.title: Intentional Reading
intentional-reading.plainText: Intentional Reading A deliberate approach to content consumption where reading time is structured into two phases: discovery (scanning for potential reads) and focused reading (highlighting, note-taking, connecting to existing knowledge). Described by YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice) as a reaction to "cope consumption" - feeling productive while passively listening to podcasts or scrolling Substack. The core shift: every piece of content read should produce new atomic-notes and connections. Pipeline Curate sources - "Who gets access to my brain?" (references @phokarlsson's "Structure your milieu") Scroll = search - browsing time is for finding potential reads to add to a backlog Read with intent - dedicated time for highlighting and note-taking, even on short articles Extract to vault - Readwise plugin pulls highlights into Obsidian, user connects them to existing notes Counter-arguments Aggressive curation may create filter bubbles and miss valuable serendipitous exposure Treating every 5-minute read as a note-taking opportunity could make reading feel like work The "cope" framing may unfairly dismiss the value of passive, exploratory consumption Data gaps No specifics on YB's reading volume or how much note output this produces "Structure your milieu" concept not ingested as a source Single source describing this in an LLM-vault context
intentional-reading.richText:
A deliberate approach to content consumption where reading time is structured into two phases: discovery (scanning for potential reads) and focused reading (highlighting, note-taking, connecting to existing knowledge).

Described by YB ({{yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice}}) as a reaction to "cope consumption" - feeling productive while passively listening to podcasts or scrolling Substack. The core shift: every piece of content read should produce new {{atomic-notes}} and connections.

##Pipeline

1. **Curate sources** - "Who gets access to my brain?" (references @phokarlsson's "Structure your milieu") 2. **Scroll = search** - browsing time is for finding potential reads to add to a backlog 3. **Read with intent** - dedicated time for highlighting and note-taking, even on short articles 4. **Extract to vault** - {{Readwise}} plugin pulls highlights into {{Obsidian}}, user connects them to existing notes

##Counter-arguments

Aggressive curation may create filter bubbles and miss valuable serendipitous exposure Treating every 5-minute read as a note-taking opportunity could make reading feel like work The "cope" framing may unfairly dismiss the value of passive, exploratory consumption

##Data gaps

No specifics on YB's reading volume or how much note output this produces "Structure your milieu" concept not ingested as a source Single source describing this in an LLM-vault context

context-compression.title: Context Compression
context-compression.plainText: Context Compression Strategies for reducing the token cost of persistent context in LLM agent workflows. As projects accumulate context files (CLAUDE.md, memory, documentation), token consumption grows with every session. Context compression addresses this without sacrificing the benefits of compound-engineering. Two Approaches File-level compression Rewrite context files into more concise language while preserving semantic meaning. Caveman Compress achieves ~46% token reduction by compressing CLAUDE.md and memory files, preserving code blocks, URLs, and structural elements while condensing prose. Tradeoff: Compressed files are harder for humans to read and maintain. The compression itself requires an API call. Retrieval-level compression (progressive disclosure) Instead of loading all context at once, provide a layered retrieval system that surfaces only relevant context on demand. Claude-Mem implements a 3-layer system (search → timeline → full details) claiming ~10x token savings. Tradeoff: Requires running additional infrastructure (databases, HTTP services). Effectiveness depends on how selectively the agent actually retrieves. Why It Matters Token costs are a direct function of context size. For practitioners running multiple folder-as-agent projects with accumulated context, costs compound. A folder-agent with a rich CLAUDE.md, documentation, and operational memory might consume thousands of tokens just for startup context before any actual work begins. Context compression is the efficiency counterpart to compound-engineering: compound engineering says accumulate context for better results; context compression says manage that accumulated context efficiently. Counter-arguments For small projects, the overhead of compression tools may exceed the savings CLAUDE.md files are typically small (1-3K tokens) - compressing them saves pennies The real cost driver is usually conversation length, not startup context "Just write concise CLAUDE.md files" may be simpler than adding tooling Data gaps No head-to-head comparison of these two approaches on the same project No data on the crossover point where compression tools pay for themselves Unknown whether compressed context degrades agent performance vs full context Both tools are early-stage - no longitudinal usage data
context-compression.richText:
Strategies for reducing the token cost of persistent context in LLM agent workflows. As projects accumulate context files (CLAUDE.md, memory, documentation), token consumption grows with every session. Context compression addresses this without sacrificing the benefits of {{compound-engineering}}.

##Two Approaches

##File-level compression

Rewrite context files into more concise language while preserving semantic meaning. {{Caveman Compress}} achieves ~46% token reduction by compressing CLAUDE.md and memory files, preserving code blocks, URLs, and structural elements while condensing prose.

**Tradeoff:** Compressed files are harder for humans to read and maintain. The compression itself requires an API call.

##Retrieval-level compression (progressive disclosure)

Instead of loading all context at once, provide a layered retrieval system that surfaces only relevant context on demand. {{Claude-Mem}} implements a 3-layer system (search → timeline → full details) claiming ~10x token savings.

**Tradeoff:** Requires running additional infrastructure (databases, HTTP services). Effectiveness depends on how selectively the agent actually retrieves.

##Why It Matters

Token costs are a direct function of context size. For practitioners running multiple {{folder-as-agent}} projects with accumulated context, costs compound. A folder-agent with a rich CLAUDE.md, documentation, and operational memory might consume thousands of tokens just for startup context before any actual work begins.

Context compression is the efficiency counterpart to {{compound-engineering}}: compound engineering says accumulate context for better results; context compression says manage that accumulated context efficiently.

##Counter-arguments

For small projects, the overhead of compression tools may exceed the savings CLAUDE.md files are typically small (1-3K tokens) - compressing them saves pennies The real cost driver is usually conversation length, not startup context "Just write concise CLAUDE.md files" may be simpler than adding tooling

##Data gaps

No head-to-head comparison of these two approaches on the same project No data on the crossover point where compression tools pay for themselves Unknown whether compressed context degrades agent performance vs full context Both tools are early-stage - no longitudinal usage data

llm-wiki-pattern.title: LLM Wiki Pattern
llm-wiki-pattern.plainText: LLM Wiki Pattern A knowledge management approach where an LLM agent reads raw sources and compiles them into structured, cross-referenced wiki pages. Distinguished from RAG by pre-compiling knowledge rather than re-deriving answers via vector search at query time. Originated by andrej-karpathy in a February 2025 tweet. At ~100 articles / ~400K words, Karpathy reported this approach outperforms RAG for personal Q&A. Key properties: Pre-compiled - knowledge is structured once, updated incrementally Compound loop - filed answers enrich future queries Bias-checked - counter-arguments and data gaps on every page Human-validated - gate prevents blind trust in AI output Research vs Personal Thinking YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice) draws a distinction: the LLM wiki pattern is "fantastic for research projects" where synthetic artifacts make sense, but for personal thinking, "you can't outsource your own life experiences to AI." This suggests the pattern works best for external knowledge compilation, while personal knowledge management needs a complementary approach built on commonplace-practice and atomic-notes. Counter-arguments Requires more upfront processing than RAG May not scale as well to very large, rapidly changing corpora Single-user testimonial, not benchmarked rigorously Data gaps No head-to-head comparison with modern RAG systems (e.g., GraphRAG) Unclear at what scale the pre-compiled approach breaks down Original Karpathy tweet/thread not yet ingested as a primary source
llm-wiki-pattern.richText:
A knowledge management approach where an LLM agent reads raw sources and compiles them into structured, cross-referenced wiki pages. Distinguished from RAG by pre-compiling knowledge rather than re-deriving answers via vector search at query time.

Originated by {{andrej-karpathy}} in a February 2025 tweet. At ~100 articles / ~400K words, Karpathy reported this approach outperforms RAG for personal Q&A.

Key properties: **Pre-compiled** - knowledge is structured once, updated incrementally **Compound loop** - filed answers enrich future queries **Bias-checked** - counter-arguments and data gaps on every page **Human-validated** - explored gate prevents blind trust in AI output

##Research vs Personal Thinking

YB ({{yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice}}) draws a distinction: the LLM wiki pattern is "fantastic for research projects" where synthetic artifacts make sense, but for personal thinking, "you can't outsource your own life experiences to AI." This suggests the pattern works best for external knowledge compilation, while personal knowledge management needs a complementary approach built on {{commonplace-practice}} and {{atomic-notes}}.

##Counter-arguments

Requires more upfront processing than RAG May not scale as well to very large, rapidly changing corpora Single-user testimonial, not benchmarked rigorously

##Data gaps

No head-to-head comparison with modern RAG systems (e.g., GraphRAG) Unclear at what scale the pre-compiled approach breaks down Original Karpathy tweet/thread not yet ingested as a primary source

keyboard-driven-workflow.title: Keyboard-Driven Workflow
keyboard-driven-workflow.plainText: Keyboard-Driven Workflow A macOS productivity philosophy where the keyboard is the primary input device, minimizing mouse/trackpad usage through layered utilities. Described by Brett Terpstra as "the most powerful tool we have when using our Macs." The key insight is that the power comes from combining specialized tools into an ecosystem, not from any single utility. Terpstra's stack covers six categories: Navigation - screen text search (Superkey), Vim bindings in any app (KindaVim), fuzzy menu search (Paletro) Key modification - system-level remapping (Karabiner-Elements), Hyper key setup, DefaultKeyBindings.dict Launching - app/file launcher (LaunchBar), static key sequences (LeaderKey) Text expansion - snippet-based (TextExpander), AI-predictive (Cotypist) Window management - keyboard-driven positioning (Moom) Web browsing - Vim navigation (Vimium), keyboard-first search (Kagi) Overlaps with the folder-as-agent pattern in one respect: both are about accumulating a personalized environment over time that compounds in effectiveness. Counter-arguments High upfront learning curve and configuration cost Tool overlap creates maintenance burden Some users may get equivalent results with fewer, more integrated tools (e.g., Raycast replacing LaunchBar + clipboard + snippets) Data gaps No structured comparison of this stack vs simpler alternatives Cost and conflict information missing Single source
keyboard-driven-workflow.richText:
A macOS productivity philosophy where the keyboard is the primary input device, minimizing mouse/trackpad usage through layered utilities. Described by {{Brett Terpstra}} as "the most powerful tool we have when using our Macs."

The key insight is that the power comes from **combining** specialized tools into an ecosystem, not from any single utility. Terpstra's stack covers six categories:

1. **Navigation** - screen text search ({{Superkey}}), Vim bindings in any app ({{KindaVim}}), fuzzy menu search ({{Paletro}}) 2. **Key modification** - system-level remapping ({{Karabiner-Elements}}), Hyper key setup, DefaultKeyBindings.dict 3. **Launching** - app/file launcher ({{LaunchBar}}), static key sequences (LeaderKey) 4. **Text expansion** - snippet-based ({{TextExpander}}), AI-predictive ({{Cotypist}}) 5. **Window management** - keyboard-driven positioning ({{Moom}}) 6. **Web browsing** - Vim navigation ({{Vimium}}), keyboard-first search ({{Kagi}})

Overlaps with the {{folder-as-agent}} pattern in one respect: both are about accumulating a personalized environment over time that compounds in effectiveness.

##Counter-arguments

High upfront learning curve and configuration cost Tool overlap creates maintenance burden Some users may get equivalent results with fewer, more integrated tools (e.g., Raycast replacing LaunchBar + clipboard + snippets)

##Data gaps

No structured comparison of this stack vs simpler alternatives Cost and conflict information missing Single source

commonplace-practice.title: Commonplace Practice
commonplace-practice.plainText: Commonplace Practice A daily habit of jotting down observations, epiphanies, thoughts, and questions. Rooted in the historical tradition of commonplace books but adapted for LLM-augmented knowledge work. In the context of a Claude + Obsidian workflow, the practice serves a dual purpose: personal reflection and building context for AI agents. The more raw personal material captured, the more an LLM can identify patterns and make connections. Key principles (per YB): Unstructured is fine - optimize for capture speed, not formatting Voice transcription preferred - captures more including uncertainty markers Context compounds - claimed benefits emerge after weeks of consistent practice Personal, not synthetic - distinguishes this from llm-wiki-pattern research workflows where AI generates the artifacts Counter-arguments Unstructured notes may produce noise that degrades LLM context quality The compounding claim rests on a single practitioner's report Historical commonplace books worked without AI - unclear what the LLM integration adds beyond search Data gaps No metrics on how much daily capture is needed for the compounding effect No comparison with structured journaling approaches Single source - needs corroboration
commonplace-practice.richText:
A daily habit of jotting down observations, epiphanies, thoughts, and questions. Rooted in the historical tradition of commonplace books but adapted for LLM-augmented knowledge work.

In the context of a Claude + {{Obsidian}} workflow, the practice serves a dual purpose: personal reflection and building context for AI agents. The more raw personal material captured, the more an LLM can identify patterns and make connections.

Key principles (per {{yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice}}):

**Unstructured is fine** - optimize for capture speed, not formatting **Voice transcription** preferred - captures more including uncertainty markers **Context compounds** - claimed benefits emerge after weeks of consistent practice **Personal, not synthetic** - distinguishes this from {{llm-wiki-pattern}} research workflows where AI generates the artifacts

##Counter-arguments

Unstructured notes may produce noise that degrades LLM context quality The compounding claim rests on a single practitioner's report Historical commonplace books worked without AI - unclear what the LLM integration adds beyond search

##Data gaps

No metrics on how much daily capture is needed for the compounding effect No comparison with structured journaling approaches Single source - needs corroboration

compound-engineering.title: Compound Engineering
compound-engineering.plainText: Compound Engineering An AI-native engineering philosophy created by Kieran Klaassen at Every.to. The core idea: context accumulated in project folders over months of work makes each subsequent AI interaction more effective. The folder-as-agent pattern is a direct product of this approach. Has 14,000+ stars on GitHub. Every.to offers a compound engineering guide and a plugin. Efficiency Counterpart: Context Compression As compound engineering accumulates context, token costs grow. Context Compression tools address this: Caveman Compress compresses the files themselves (~46% reduction), while Claude-Mem adds progressive disclosure to avoid loading everything (~10x savings). These are the efficiency layer that makes sustained compound engineering economically viable. Counter-arguments "Accumulated context improves results" is intuitive but hard to measure May conflate project maturity with compound engineering benefits Data gaps Source article mentions compound engineering but doesn't explain the methodology in detail The GitHub guide and plugin not yet ingested as primary sources No comparison with non-compound approaches on the same project
compound-engineering.richText:
An AI-native engineering philosophy created by {{Kieran Klaassen}} at Every.to. The core idea: context accumulated in project folders over months of work makes each subsequent AI interaction more effective. The {{folder-as-agent}} pattern is a direct product of this approach.

Has 14,000+ stars on GitHub. Every.to offers a compound engineering guide and a plugin.

##Efficiency Counterpart: Context Compression

As compound engineering accumulates context, token costs grow. {{Context Compression}} tools address this: {{Caveman Compress}} compresses the files themselves (~46% reduction), while {{Claude-Mem}} adds progressive disclosure to avoid loading everything (~10x savings). These are the efficiency layer that makes sustained compound engineering economically viable.

##Counter-arguments

"Accumulated context improves results" is intuitive but hard to measure May conflate project maturity with compound engineering benefits

##Data gaps

Source article mentions compound engineering but doesn't explain the methodology in detail The GitHub guide and plugin not yet ingested as primary sources No comparison with non-compound approaches on the same project

knowledge-work-prs.title: Knowledge Work PRs
knowledge-work-prs.plainText: Knowledge Work PRs A workflow pattern where LLM agent subroutines process vault content and propose changes that the human reviews, analogous to code pull requests. Described by YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice) as the culmination of the Claude + Obsidian setup, but only effective if the preceding practices (commonplace-practice, atomic-notes, intentional-reading, agentic-constitution) are in place. How it works User defines subroutines (instruction sets) in the vault Each subroutine starts by reading the agentic-constitution Agent executes the subroutine and drafts changes User reviews changes one-by-one in a voice session (approve/tweak/reject) Approved changes get committed to the vault Example: "Tend the Vault" - checks daily commonplace-practice entries since last run, identifies threads and insights, drafts updates to atomic-notes, presents changes for review. The metaphor: agents are developers, you are the engineering manager reviewing PRs before they hit production. Relationship to Folder-as-Agent Kieran Klaassen's folder-as-agent pattern describes a similar human-in-the-loop dynamic at the engineering level: parallel Claude Code sessions work when each agent has a clear task and a human directs the work, but agent swarms coordinating autonomously fail. Knowledge Work PRs formalize this same constraint for personal knowledge management - the agent proposes, the human disposes. Counter-arguments The PR metaphor may break down - knowledge work changes are harder to diff than code Heavy reliance on agent quality - poor drafts waste more review time than they save Voice review sessions add friction compared to just writing the notes yourself Data gaps No subroutine examples shown (only "Tend the Vault" described at high level) No data on approval/rejection rates or time savings Klaassen's folder-as-agent article provides indirect corroboration of the human-review pattern
knowledge-work-prs.richText:
A workflow pattern where LLM agent subroutines process vault content and propose changes that the human reviews, analogous to code pull requests.

Described by YB ({{yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice}}) as the culmination of the Claude + {{Obsidian}} setup, but only effective if the preceding practices ({{commonplace-practice}}, {{atomic-notes}}, {{intentional-reading}}, {{agentic-constitution}}) are in place.

##How it works

1. User defines subroutines (instruction sets) in the vault 2. Each subroutine starts by reading the {{agentic-constitution}} 3. Agent executes the subroutine and drafts changes 4. User reviews changes one-by-one in a voice session (approve/tweak/reject) 5. Approved changes get committed to the vault

**Example**: "Tend the Vault" - checks daily {{commonplace-practice}} entries since last run, identifies threads and insights, drafts updates to {{atomic-notes}}, presents changes for review.

The metaphor: agents are developers, you are the engineering manager reviewing PRs before they hit production.

##Relationship to Folder-as-Agent

{{Kieran Klaassen}}'s {{folder-as-agent}} pattern describes a similar human-in-the-loop dynamic at the engineering level: parallel {{Claude Code}} sessions work when each agent has a clear task and a human directs the work, but agent swarms coordinating autonomously fail. Knowledge Work PRs formalize this same constraint for personal knowledge management - the agent proposes, the human disposes.

##Counter-arguments

The PR metaphor may break down - knowledge work changes are harder to diff than code Heavy reliance on agent quality - poor drafts waste more review time than they save Voice review sessions add friction compared to just writing the notes yourself

##Data gaps

No subroutine examples shown (only "Tend the Vault" described at high level) No data on approval/rejection rates or time savings Klaassen's folder-as-agent article provides indirect corroboration of the human-review pattern

atomic-notes.title: Atomic Notes
atomic-notes.plainText: Atomic Notes A note-taking practice where each note captures a single concept rather than facts, names, or dates. Attributed to Andy Matuschak's evergreen notes practice, itself building on Niklas Luhmann's Zettelkasten method. YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice) frames atomic notes as "API endpoints you call when solving problems" - reusable conceptual building blocks that can be combined across life domains you'd normally keep separate. In an LLM-augmented vault, atomic notes serve as the structured layer that makes commonplace-practice captures useful. Once enough atomic notes exist, you can dictate a train of thought and the AI pulls relevant past thinking from across the vault. Connects to Paul Graham's observation that "writing is thinking" - the act of distilling a concept into an atomic note forces clarity. Counter-arguments Atomicity is subjective - practitioners disagree on the right granularity The "API endpoint" metaphor may overstate how cleanly concepts decompose Overhead of maintaining many small notes can become its own burden Data gaps Matuschak's original writing not yet ingested as a primary source No evidence on optimal note count before LLM recall becomes useful Single source describing the practice in an LLM context
atomic-notes.richText:
A note-taking practice where each note captures a single concept rather than facts, names, or dates. Attributed to {{Andy Matuschak}}'s evergreen notes practice, itself building on Niklas Luhmann's Zettelkasten method.

YB ({{yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice}}) frames atomic notes as "API endpoints you call when solving problems" - reusable conceptual building blocks that can be combined across life domains you'd normally keep separate.

In an LLM-augmented vault, atomic notes serve as the structured layer that makes {{commonplace-practice}} captures useful. Once enough atomic notes exist, you can dictate a train of thought and the AI pulls relevant past thinking from across the vault.

Connects to {{Paul Graham}}'s observation that "writing is thinking" - the act of distilling a concept into an atomic note forces clarity.

##Counter-arguments

Atomicity is subjective - practitioners disagree on the right granularity The "API endpoint" metaphor may overstate how cleanly concepts decompose Overhead of maintaining many small notes can become its own burden

##Data gaps

agentic-constitution.title: Agentic Constitution
agentic-constitution.plainText: Agentic Constitution A file that serves as the bridge between an Obsidian vault and Claude (or other LLM agents). Contains high-level personal context: who you are, your big question, how you like to work, main goals, current problems, team context. Described by YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice) as the first file agents read before doing any work, ensuring alignment with the user's current priorities and thinking style. Key characteristics: Living document - updated weekly as priorities shift Team-like communication - treat it as briefing a team member, not configuring software Portal function - the primary interface between personal knowledge and AI agents Feeds subroutines - all knowledge-work-prs start by reading this file Functionally similar to a project instruction file, but scoped to personal identity and goals rather than codebase conventions. Relationship to Folder-as-Agent Kieran Klaassen (klaassen-2026-folder-is-the-agent) describes a related pattern at the project level: the CLAUDE.md file in a project folder, combined with accumulated documentation and operational memory, turns the folder itself into an agent. Where the agentic constitution captures personal-level context (identity, goals, work style), the folder-as-agent captures project-level context (code conventions, architecture, runbooks). Both converge on the principle that accumulated context is the agent. Claude Cowork "Brain File" JJ Englert (englert-2026-claude-cowork-tutorial) demonstrates the same pattern in Claude Cowork: a preferences document (the "brain file") that Claude reads at the start of every session. Contains who you are, how you work, communication style, and priorities. This is the agentic constitution applied within Cowork's project-based UI, confirming the pattern extends beyond developer-oriented tools to mainstream productivity use cases. Three independent sources now describe variations of this pattern: personal vault context (YB), project folder context (Klaassen), and Cowork project preferences (Englert). Counter-arguments Weekly updates are a significant maintenance burden for unclear returns Risk of AI over-fitting to stated goals and filtering out serendipitous discoveries The distinction from a standard system prompt or CLAUDE.md is unclear Data gaps No example of what a good agentic constitution looks like (YB references a template in replies but content not captured) Now referenced by three sources (YB, Klaassen, Englert) across personal, project, and productivity contexts No data on how much constitution detail actually improves agent output quality
agentic-constitution.richText:

##Relationship to Folder-as-Agent

{{Kieran Klaassen}} ({{klaassen-2026-folder-is-the-agent}}) describes a related pattern at the project level: the CLAUDE.md file in a project folder, combined with accumulated documentation and operational memory, turns the folder itself into an agent. Where the agentic constitution captures personal-level context (identity, goals, work style), the folder-as-agent captures project-level context (code conventions, architecture, runbooks). Both converge on the principle that **accumulated context is the agent**.

##Claude Cowork "Brain File"

{{JJ Englert}} ({{englert-2026-claude-cowork-tutorial}}) demonstrates the same pattern in {{Claude Cowork}}: a preferences document (the "brain file") that Claude reads at the start of every session. folder-as-agent.title: Folder-as-Agent Pattern
folder-as-agent.plainText: Folder-as-Agent Pattern A design pattern where a project folder - containing instruction files (CLAUDE.md/AGENT.md), skill definitions, documentation, and accumulated operational context - transforms a generalized LLM into a specialist agent. Coined by Kieran Klaassen in the context of building Cora at Every.to. Core claim: An agent is a model with enough context so you don't have to re-explain everything each time you open the chat. The folder provides that context. The same model pointed at different folders becomes a fundamentally different agent. Layers of a Folder-Agent Conventions - coding standards, deploy workflows, naming patterns (CLAUDE.md) Institutional knowledge - architecture docs, design decisions, domain documentation Operational memory - runbooks, incident investigations, resolved bugs Specialized sub-agents - refined instruction files for specific tasks (reviewers, planners) Relationship to Other Patterns Agentic Constitution - similar principle applied at the personal level rather than project level. The constitution captures identity and goals; the folder captures project knowledge. LLM Wiki Pattern - another form of pre-compiled context, but organized as a queryable knowledge base rather than an agent instruction set. Compound Engineering - Klaassen's broader methodology; the folder-as-agent pattern is one outcome of sustained compound engineering practice. Claude Cowork as Folder-Agent UI JJ Englert (englert-2026-claude-cowork-tutorial) demonstrates the folder-as-agent pattern in Claude Cowork: each project maps to a folder on your computer, with shared memory persisting across conversations. The project accumulates context (brain files, skills, connector configurations) that make Claude progressively more effective - the same compound accumulation Klaassen describes, but packaged in a GUI for non-engineers. Anti-pattern: Agent Swarms Klaassen explicitly warns against agent swarms - multiple agents coordinating with each other without human direction. The failure mode: agents don't have a speed limit, but the human reviewing their output does. Parallel sessions work only when each agent has a clear task and a human directs the work. Counter-arguments "Give the model good context" is a well-known principle - the folder framing adds packaging but not necessarily new insight The 44-agent claim is unverified and the dispatch layer is undescribed Agent swarm failures may reflect 2026 tooling limitations, not a permanent constraint Data gaps Source article clipping is truncated - dispatch layer details missing No examples of what a minimal vs mature folder-agent looks like No metrics on how folder-agent quality improves over time Now corroborated by Englert's Cowork tutorial, though in a different (GUI) context
folder-as-agent.richText:
A design pattern where a project folder - containing instruction files (CLAUDE.md/AGENT.md), skill definitions, documentation, and accumulated operational context - transforms a generalized LLM into a specialist agent. Coined by {{Kieran Klaassen}} in the context of building {{Cora}} at Every.to.

**Core claim**: An agent is a model with enough context so you don't have to re-explain everything each time you open the chat. The folder provides that context. The same model pointed at different folders becomes a fundamentally different agent.

##Layers of a Folder-Agent

1. **Conventions** - coding standards, deploy workflows, naming patterns (CLAUDE.md) 2. **Institutional knowledge** - architecture docs, design decisions, domain documentation 3. **Operational memory** - runbooks, incident investigations, resolved bugs 4. **Specialized sub-agents** - refined instruction files for specific tasks (reviewers, planners)

##Relationship to Other Patterns

**{{Agentic Constitution}}** - similar principle applied at the personal level rather than project level. The constitution captures identity and goals; the folder captures project knowledge. **{{LLM Wiki Pattern}}** - another form of pre-compiled context, but organized as a queryable knowledge base rather than an agent instruction set. **{{Compound Engineering}}** - Klaassen's broader methodology; the folder-as-agent pattern is one outcome of sustained compound engineering practice.

##Claude Cowork as Folder-Agent UI

{{JJ Englert}} ({{englert-2026-claude-cowork-tutorial}}) demonstrates the folder-as-agent pattern in {{Claude Cowork}}: each project maps to a folder on your computer, with shared memory persisting across conversations. The project accumulates context (brain files, skills, connector configurations) that make Claude progressively more effective - the same compound accumulation Klaassen describes, but packaged in a GUI for non-engineers.

##Anti-pattern: Agent Swarms

Klaassen explicitly warns against agent swarms - multiple agents coordinating with each other without human direction. The failure mode: agents don't have a speed limit, but the human reviewing their output does. Parallel sessions work only when each agent has a clear task and a human directs the work.

##Counter-arguments

"Give the model good context" is a well-known principle - the folder framing adds packaging but not necessarily new insight The 44-agent claim is unverified and the dispatch layer is undescribed Agent swarm failures may reflect 2026 tooling limitations, not a permanent constraint

##Data gaps

Source article clipping is truncated - dispatch layer details missing No examples of what a minimal vs mature folder-agent looks like No metrics on how folder-agent quality improves over time Now corroborated by Englert's Cowork tutorial, though in a different (GUI) context

yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice.title: YB - Build a Daily Commonplace Practice
yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice.plainText: YB - Build a Daily Commonplace Practice Source: Tweet thread by YB (@yb_effect), 2026-04-13 Engagement: 129 likes, 279 bookmarks, 6.1K impressions !yb-effect-commonplace-practice-overview.jpg The image summarizes the five practices and recommended Obsidian plugins (Copilot, Dataview, Readwise, Excalidraw, Simple Note Review). The thread distinguishes this personal thinking use case from andrej-karpathy's llm-wiki-pattern, which YB considers better suited for research projects with synthetic artifacts. The key difference: for personal thinking, you can't outsource your own life experiences to AI - the personal context you add is what makes the tool useful. Voice transcription recommended because it captures more context, including uncertainty ("ifs, buts, and ums"). YB frames them as "API endpoints you call when solving problems." Thinking in atomic notes lets you pull context from different life domains you'd normally keep separate. References Paul Graham's "writing is thinking" - write notes for yourself, not for an audience. The payoff: once you have enough atomic notes, you can dictate a train of thought and Claude pulls your own past thinking to help. Intentional Reading (via Readwise) Connects Readwise or Obsidian Web Clipper to build a reading pipeline. YB's key insight: much past content consumption was "cope" - feeling productive while passively scrolling. The new approach: search for potential reads during scroll time, then dedicate focused time to reading with highlighting and note-taking. Even a 5-minute article should produce new atomic notes. References @phokarlsson's "Structure your milieu" concept - deliberately curate who gets access to your brain. Agentic Constitution A file that serves as the portal between Obsidian and Claude. Contains high-level context: who you are, your big question, how you like to work, main goals, current problems, team context. Agents read this file first before doing any work. Key characteristic: it's a constant WIP document, updated weekly, treated like updating a team on changing priorities. Knowledge Work PRs Vault subroutines - specific instruction sets that start by reading the agentic constitution. Example: "Tend the Vault" checks daily commonplace entries, identifies threads, drafts updates to atomic notes. The user then reviews changes in a voice session, approving/tweaking/rejecting, similar to a code PR review process. Mentioned Tools & People Andrej Karpathy - LLM wiki pattern (research-oriented) Andy Matuschak - atomic note-taking practice Paul Graham - "writing is thinking" Readwise - reading highlights pipeline @phokarlsson - "Structure your milieu" concept @kepano - Obsidian Web Clipper Counter-arguments The 9-month claim is a single practitioner's experience with no controlled comparison Voice transcription produces messy notes that may create noise rather than signal for LLMs The "context compounds" claim is unfalsifiable - confirmation bias could explain perceived patterns Heavy setup cost and daily habit requirement may not suit most people Data gaps No specifics on what "surprising patterns" the system surfaced No comparison of outcomes vs simpler note-taking approaches The "Tend the Vault" subroutine and other agent workflows are described but not shown No discussion of failure modes or when the system breaks down
yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice.richText:
**Source**: Tweet thread by YB (@yb_effect), 2026-04-13 **Engagement**: 129 likes, 279 bookmarks, 6.1K impressions

!{{yb-effect-commonplace-practice-overview.jpg}}

The image summarizes the five practices and recommended {{Obsidian}} plugins (Copilot, Dataview, {{Readwise}}, Excalidraw, Simple Note Review).

##Summary

YB describes a Claude + {{Obsidian}} workflow they've used for 9+ months, arguing it fundamentally changed their daily habits. The thread distinguishes this personal thinking use case from {{andrej-karpathy}}'s {{llm-wiki-pattern}}, which YB considers better suited for research projects with synthetic artifacts. The key difference: for personal thinking, you can't outsource your own life experiences to AI - the personal context you add is what makes the tool useful.

##Five Practices

##1. {{Commonplace Practice}}

Daily habit of jotting down observations, epiphanies, thoughts, and questions. Notes don't need to be clean or structured. Voice transcription recommended because it captures more context, including uncertainty ("ifs, buts, and ums"). The claim: context compounds over weeks and months, producing surprising pattern recognition.

##2. {{Atomic Notes}} (via {{Andy Matuschak}})

Notes should be concepts, not facts/names/dates. YB frames them as "API endpoints you call when solving problems." Thinking in atomic notes lets you pull context from different life domains you'd normally keep separate. References {{Paul Graham}}'s "writing is thinking" - write notes for yourself, not for an audience.

The payoff: once you have enough atomic notes, you can dictate a train of thought and Claude pulls your own past thinking to help.

##3. {{Intentional Reading}} (via {{Readwise}})

Connects {{Readwise}} or Obsidian Web Clipper to build a reading pipeline. YB's key insight: much past content consumption was "cope" - feeling productive while passively scrolling. The new approach: search for potential reads during scroll time, then dedicate focused time to reading with highlighting and note-taking. Even a 5-minute article should produce new atomic notes.

References @phokarlsson's "Structure your milieu" concept - deliberately curate who gets access to your brain.

##4. {{Agentic Constitution}}

A .md file that serves as the portal between {{Obsidian}} and Claude. Contains high-level context: who you are, your big question, how you like to work, main goals, current problems, team context. Agents read this file first before doing any work.

Key characteristic: it's a constant WIP document, updated weekly, treated like updating a team on changing priorities.

##5. {{Knowledge Work PRs}}

Vault subroutines - specific instruction sets that start by reading the {{agentic constitution}}. Example: "Tend the Vault" checks daily commonplace entries, identifies threads, drafts updates to atomic notes. The user then reviews changes in a voice session, approving/tweaking/rejecting, similar to a code PR review process.

##Mentioned Tools & People

{{Andrej Karpathy}} - LLM wiki pattern (research-oriented) {{Andy Matuschak}} - atomic note-taking practice {{Paul Graham}} - "writing is thinking" {{Readwise}} - reading highlights pipeline @phokarlsson - "Structure your milieu" concept @kepano - Obsidian Web Clipper

##Counter-arguments

The 9-month claim is a single practitioner's experience with no controlled comparison Voice transcription produces messy notes that may create noise rather than signal for LLMs The "context compounds" claim is unfalsifiable - confirmation bias could explain perceived patterns Heavy setup cost and daily habit requirement may not suit most people

##Data gaps

No specifics on what "surprising patterns" the system surfaced No comparison of outcomes vs simpler note-taking approaches The "Tend the Vault" subroutine and other agent workflows are described but not shown No discussion of failure modes or when the system breaks down

newman-2026-claude-mem.title: Alex Newman - Claude-Mem
newman-2026-claude-mem.plainText: Alex Newman - Claude-Mem Source: GitHub README Author: Alex Newman (@thedotmack) Format: Open-source Claude Code plugin License: AGPL-3.0 Summary Claude-Mem is a persistent memory compression system for Claude Code that captures what Claude learns during tool interactions, compresses observations into semantic summaries, and makes them available to future sessions. It addresses context-compression through progressive disclosure rather than file compression. Architecture Lifecycle hooks tie into Claude Code events: SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse, Stop, SessionEnd Worker service runs on port 37777 with HTTP API and web viewer UI (Bun runtime) Storage uses SQLite with FTS5 full-text search plus Chroma vector database for hybrid semantic/keyword search 3-Layer Progressive Disclosure The key design: instead of dumping all context at once, retrieval happens in three layers with ~10x token savings: search - compact index with IDs (~50-100 tokens per result) timeline - chronological context around observations get_observations - full details for filtered IDs (~500-1,000 tokens per result) This lets Claude selectively load only the context it needs rather than consuming tokens on irrelevant history. Relationship to Other Tools Solves the same problem as Caveman Compress (reducing token costs for persistent context) but from the opposite direction: claude-mem adds a retrieval layer so you don't load everything, while caveman compresses the files themselves. Both are context-compression strategies. Also related to compound-engineering: claude-mem is infrastructure for accumulating project knowledge over time, which is the core promise of compound engineering. Advanced Features Endless Mode - biomimetic memory for extended sessions Privacy controls - tags exclude sensitive content Real-time feeds - optional streaming to Telegram, Discord, Slack Cross-platform: supports Claude Code, Gemini CLI, OpenClaw Counter-arguments Running a local HTTP service + SQLite + Chroma is heavy infrastructure for memory management Lifecycle hooks add latency to every tool interaction 10x token savings claim depends on how selectively the agent actually retrieves - a lazy agent might still pull everything AGPL-3.0 license limits commercial use Data gaps No benchmarks showing actual token savings in real workflows No comparison with simpler approaches (e.g., just a well-maintained CLAUDE.md) Unclear how well the vector search handles cross-session concept drift No data on storage growth rate over long-running projects
newman-2026-claude-mem.richText:
**Source:** GitHub README **Author:** Alex Newman (@thedotmack) **Format:** Open-source Claude Code plugin **License:** AGPL-3.0

##Summary

{{Claude-Mem}} is a persistent memory compression system for Claude Code that captures what Claude learns during tool interactions, compresses observations into semantic summaries, and makes them available to future sessions. It addresses {{context-compression}} through progressive disclosure rather than file compression.

##Architecture

**Lifecycle hooks** tie into Claude Code events: SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse, Stop, SessionEnd **Worker service** runs on port 37777 with HTTP API and web viewer UI (Bun runtime) **Storage** uses SQLite with FTS5 full-text search plus Chroma vector database for hybrid semantic/keyword search

##3-Layer Progressive Disclosure

The key design: instead of dumping all context at once, retrieval happens in three layers with ~10x token savings:

1. **search** - compact index with IDs (~50-100 tokens per result) 2. **timeline** - chronological context around observations 3. **get_observations** - full details for filtered IDs (~500-1,000 tokens per result)

This lets Claude selectively load only the context it needs rather than consuming tokens on irrelevant history.

##Relationship to Other Tools

Solves the same problem as {{Caveman Compress}} (reducing token costs for persistent context) but from the opposite direction: claude-mem adds a retrieval layer so you don't load everything, while caveman compresses the files themselves. Both are {{context-compression}} strategies.

Also related to {{compound-engineering}}: claude-mem is infrastructure for accumulating project knowledge over time, which is the core promise of compound engineering.

##Advanced Features

**Endless Mode** - biomimetic memory for extended sessions **Privacy controls** - <private> tags exclude sensitive content **Real-time feeds** - optional streaming to Telegram, Discord, Slack Cross-platform: supports Claude Code, Gemini CLI, OpenClaw

##Counter-arguments

Running a local HTTP service + SQLite + Chroma is heavy infrastructure for memory management Lifecycle hooks add latency to every tool interaction 10x token savings claim depends on how selectively the agent actually retrieves - a lazy agent might still pull everything AGPL-3.0 license limits commercial use

##Data gaps

No benchmarks showing actual token savings in real workflows No comparison with simpler approaches (e.g., just a well-maintained CLAUDE.md) Unclear how well the vector search handles cross-session concept drift No data on storage growth rate over long-running projects

klaassen-2026-folder-is-the-agent.title: Kieran Klaassen - The Folder Is the Agent
klaassen-2026-folder-is-the-agent.plainText: Kieran Klaassen - The Folder Is the Agent Source: Article by Kieran Klaassen, Every.to, 2026-04-13 Context: Klaassen is general manager of Cora (Every's AI email assistant) and creator of the compound-engineering methodology (14,000+ GitHub stars). Summary Klaassen spent three months trying to make agent swarms work - multiple AI agents coordinating with each other, deciding what to work on, producing output he hadn't shaped. The experiment failed: when 10 agents finished simultaneously, he had 10 results to evaluate without enough context to trust any of them. The insight: "AI agents don't have a speed limit, but the person managing them still does." He stopped looking for a smarter orchestration layer and realized the real agent was something he already had - a project folder. A folder with a CLAUDE.md/AGENT.md file, skill definitions, and context accumulated through months of compound-engineering makes a generalized model into a specialist. The Folder-as-Agent Pattern An agent is defined as: a model with enough context so you don't have to re-explain everything each time you open the chat. The ~/cora/ folder demonstrates the layers: Conventions and standards - CLAUDE.md covers Rails conventions, deploy workflows, database patterns Institutional knowledge - docs/developer-docs/ holds architecture reports, email processing pipeline, assistant system design Operational memory - docs/runbooks/ and docs/investigations/ capture patterns from real incidents Specialized agents - .claude/agents/ holds refined specialists: reviewers, planners, component creators Reading order for new agents: CLAUDE.md first, then architecture document, then assistant system report, then assistant prompt, then component creator agent. Key Claims Klaassen runs 44 folder-agents across multiple projects, each in a specialized folder built over months A dispatch layer routes between the 44 agents Claude Code Skills - files that give the model specific capabilities - are an example of the folder-as-agent structure. People were already writing markdown instruction files and dropping them into project directories before anyone called them "skills" Parallel Claude Code sessions work when each agent has a clear task and a human directs work; swarms where agents coordinate with each other fail The same model (mostly Claude Opus 4.6, also GPT 5.4 and Gemini Pro 3.1) becomes a completely different agent when pointed at different folders Relationship to Other Concepts The folder-as-agent pattern is closely related to the agentic-constitution concept from YB - both treat structured markdown files as the primary interface between human intent and AI capability. The key difference: Klaassen's pattern emphasizes project-level accumulated context (codebases, runbooks, incident history), while YB's constitution emphasizes personal-level context (identity, goals, work style). Both patterns converge on the idea that the value is in the accumulated context, not the model itself. Mentioned Tools and People Claude Code - primary tool for running folder-agents Cora - Every's AI email assistant, Klaassen's main project compound-engineering - Klaassen's AI-native engineering methodology Every.to - publisher, where Klaassen builds Cora Trevin Chow - product leader working with Klaassen on compound engineering Counter-arguments 44 agents sounds impressive but the article doesn't describe what most of them do or how the dispatch layer works (clipping truncated) The "folder is the agent" framing is essentially "give the model good context" - a well-known principle repackaged as a methodology The failure of agent swarms may reflect the state of tooling in early 2026, not a fundamental limitation No discussion of when folder-agents fail or produce poor output Data gaps Article clipping is truncated - likely missing sections on the dispatch layer, specific agent examples, and conclusions No metrics on productivity gains from the 44-agent setup vs fewer agents The dispatch layer between agents is mentioned but not described No comparison with other orchestration approaches that did work
klaassen-2026-folder-is-the-agent.richText:
**Source**: Article by Kieran Klaassen, Every.to, 2026-04-13 **Context**: Klaassen is general manager of {{Cora}} (Every's AI email assistant) and creator of the {{compound-engineering}} methodology (14,000+ GitHub stars).

##Summary

Klaassen spent three months trying to make agent swarms work - multiple AI agents coordinating with each other, deciding what to work on, producing output he hadn't shaped. The experiment failed: when 10 agents finished simultaneously, he had 10 results to evaluate without enough context to trust any of them. The insight: "AI agents don't have a speed limit, but the person managing them still does."

He stopped looking for a smarter orchestration layer and realized the real agent was something he already had - a **project folder**. A folder with a CLAUDE.md/AGENT.md file, skill definitions, and context accumulated through months of {{compound-engineering}} makes a generalized model into a specialist.

##The Folder-as-Agent Pattern

An agent is defined as: **a model with enough context so you don't have to re-explain everything each time you open the chat.**

The ~/cora/ folder demonstrates the layers:

**Conventions and standards** - CLAUDE.md covers Rails conventions, deploy workflows, database patterns **Institutional knowledge** - docs/developer-docs/ holds architecture reports, email processing pipeline, assistant system design **Operational memory** - docs/runbooks/ and docs/investigations/ capture patterns from real incidents **Specialized agents** - .claude/agents/ holds refined specialists: reviewers, planners, component creators

Reading order for new agents: CLAUDE.md first, then architecture document, then assistant system report, then assistant prompt, then component creator agent.

##Key Claims

Klaassen runs **44 folder-agents** across multiple projects, each in a specialized folder built over months A **dispatch layer** routes between the 44 agents {{Claude Code}} Skills - files that give the model specific capabilities - are an example of the folder-as-agent structure. People were already writing markdown instruction files and dropping them into project directories before anyone called them "skills" Parallel Claude Code sessions work when each agent has a clear task and a human directs work; swarms where agents coordinate with each other fail The same model (mostly {{Claude}} Opus 4.6, also GPT 5.4 and Gemini Pro 3.1) becomes a completely different agent when pointed at different folders

##Relationship to Other Concepts

The folder-as-agent pattern is closely related to the {{agentic-constitution}} concept from YB - both treat structured markdown files as the primary interface between human intent and AI capability. The key difference: Klaassen's pattern emphasizes **project-level** accumulated context (codebases, runbooks, incident history), while YB's constitution emphasizes **personal-level** context (identity, goals, work style).

Both patterns converge on the idea that the value is in the **accumulated context**, not the model itself.

##Mentioned Tools and People

{{Claude Code}} - primary tool for running folder-agents {{Cora}} - Every's AI email assistant, Klaassen's main project {{compound-engineering}} - Klaassen's AI-native engineering methodology Every.to - publisher, where Klaassen builds Cora Trevin Chow - product leader working with Klaassen on compound engineering

##Counter-arguments

44 agents sounds impressive but the article doesn't describe what most of them do or how the dispatch layer works (clipping truncated) The "folder is the agent" framing is essentially "give the model good context" - a well-known principle repackaged as a methodology The failure of agent swarms may reflect the state of tooling in early 2026, not a fundamental limitation No discussion of when folder-agents fail or produce poor output

##Data gaps

Article clipping is truncated - likely missing sections on the dispatch layer, specific agent examples, and conclusions No metrics on productivity gains from the 44-agent setup vs fewer agents The dispatch layer between agents is mentioned but not described No comparison with other orchestration approaches that did work

terpstra-2026-keyboard-driven-mac-utilit.title: Brett Terpstra - Keyboard-Driven Mac Utility List
terpstra-2026-keyboard-driven-mac-utilit.plainText: Brett Terpstra - Keyboard-Driven Mac Utility List Source: Blog post by Brett Terpstra, 2026-03-27 Context: Terpstra is a developer and writer focused on macOS productivity tooling. Summary A curated overview of 20 macOS utilities organized around the principle that the keyboard is the most powerful input tool on a Mac. Terpstra uses all of these daily and describes how they complement each other. Categories and Tools Keyboard Navigation Superkey - searches text on screen, simulates mouse click on matching text. Navigate without leaving keyboard. Wooshy - alternative to Superkey for navigating all UI elements via keyboard. KindaVim - brings Vim-like key bindings (h/j/k/l/u/d/a/i) to any macOS app. Not perfect but great for basic Vim navigation. Paletro - Shift+Cmd+P in any app for fuzzy menu item search. Used daily. Scrolla - keyboard-driven scrolling through documents. Keyboard Modifications BetterTouchTool - trackpad gesture and keyboard shortcut manager. Creates personalized commands. Karabiner-Elements - system-level key remapping. Terpstra uses it for Hyper key setup. DefaultKeyBindings.dict - macOS built-in key mapping file for custom keyboard shortcuts. Keyboard Maestro - automation engine for repetitive tasks. Combined with key bindings for streamlined text editing. Launchers LaunchBar - Terpstra's preferred launcher after trying all alternatives. Features: clipboard history across reboots, Instant Send (double-tap Option), file actions. Combined with KindaVim and Superkey, creates a powerful navigation system. LeaderKey - static single-key sequences for launching frequently used apps and URLs. Complements LaunchBar. Text Expansion TextExpander - longtime tool with snippet search, reminders, shell script execution. Expansion after whitespace feature gives it an edge. TextBlaze - alternative text expander, but Terpstra finds using both confusing. Cotypist - current favorite. AI-powered automatic text completion in any app. Predicts inputs without pre-created snippets. Works in Cursor too. Window Management Moom - move and resize windows via keyboard. Grid mode for drawing positions on the fly. Web Browsing Vimium - Vim-style navigation for browsers. Press F to tag visible links with key sequences. Kagi - search engine with full keyboard navigation and AI slop filtering. Key Insights The tools form an ecosystem where each fills a gap the others leave. The power is in the combination, not individual tools. Terpstra mentions an unpublished script based on NiftyMenu that scans any app for available shortcuts and generates Dash cheatsheets. The LaunchBar + KindaVim + Superkey combination is highlighted as particularly powerful for mouse-free navigation. Counter-arguments 20 utilities is a heavy investment in configuration and muscle memory Many tools overlap in functionality (e.g., BetterTouchTool can do some of what Keyboard Maestro and Karabiner do) Using both TextExpander and TextBlaze simultaneously suggests even the author hasn't fully rationalized the stack Some tools (Cotypist) rely on AI prediction which may not suit all writing styles Data gaps No discussion of how long it took to build this workflow or the learning curve No mention of conflicts between tools (key binding collisions, etc.) Missing cost breakdown - several are paid apps No comparison with built-in macOS keyboard features that might replace some of these tools

##Summary

A curated overview of 20 macOS utilities organized around the principle that the keyboard is the most powerful input tool on a Mac. Terpstra uses all of these daily and describes how they complement each other.

##Categories and Tools

##Keyboard Navigation

**{{Superkey}}** - searches text on screen, simulates mouse click on matching text. Navigate without leaving keyboard. **Wooshy** - alternative to Superkey for navigating all UI elements via keyboard. **{{KindaVim}}** - brings Vim-like key bindings (h/j/k/l/u/d/a/i) to any macOS app. Not perfect but great for basic Vim navigation. **{{Paletro}}** - Shift+Cmd+P in any app for fuzzy menu item search. Used daily. **Scrolla** - keyboard-driven scrolling through documents.

##Keyboard Modifications

**{{BetterTouchTool}}** - trackpad gesture and keyboard shortcut manager. Creates personalized commands. **{{Karabiner-Elements}}** - system-level key remapping. Terpstra uses it for Hyper key setup. **DefaultKeyBindings.dict** - macOS built-in key mapping file for custom keyboard shortcuts. **{{Keyboard Maestro}}** - automation engine for repetitive tasks. Combined with key bindings for streamlined text editing.

##Launchers

**{{LaunchBar}}** - Terpstra's preferred launcher after trying all alternatives. Features: clipboard history across reboots, Instant Send (double-tap Option), file actions. Combined with KindaVim and Superkey, creates a powerful navigation system. **LeaderKey** - static single-key sequences for launching frequently used apps and URLs. Complements LaunchBar.

##Text Expansion

**{{TextExpander}}** - longtime tool with snippet search, reminders, shell script execution. Expansion after whitespace feature gives it an edge. **TextBlaze** - alternative text expander, but Terpstra finds using both confusing. **{{Cotypist}}** - current favorite. AI-powered automatic text completion in any app. Predicts inputs without pre-created snippets. Works in Cursor too.

##Window Management

**{{Moom}}** - move and resize windows via keyboard. Grid mode for drawing positions on the fly.

##Web Browsing

**{{Vimium}}** - Vim-style navigation for browsers. Press F to tag visible links with key sequences. **{{Kagi}}** - search engine with full keyboard navigation and AI slop filtering.

##Key Insights

The tools form an ecosystem where each fills a gap the others leave. The power is in the combination, not individual tools. Terpstra mentions an unpublished script based on NiftyMenu that scans any app for available shortcuts and generates {{Dash}} cheatsheets. The LaunchBar + KindaVim + Superkey combination is highlighted as particularly powerful for mouse-free navigation.

##Counter-arguments

20 utilities is a heavy investment in configuration and muscle memory Many tools overlap in functionality (e.g., BetterTouchTool can do some of what Keyboard Maestro and Karabiner do) Using both TextExpander and TextBlaze simultaneously suggests even the author hasn't fully rationalized the stack Some tools (Cotypist) rely on AI prediction which may not suit all writing styles

##Data gaps

No discussion of how long it took to build this workflow or the learning curve No mention of conflicts between tools (key binding collisions, etc.) Missing cost breakdown - several are paid apps No comparison with built-in macOS keyboard features that might replace some of these tools
